data.title: Sprinklr Analyst Highlights Strong Q2, Raised Outlook But Flags Leadership Shifts And Churn Risks
data.description:
Sprinklr CXM posted second-quarter revenue of $212 million, up 8% year-over-year and ahead of guidance. Subscriptions and professional services led growth. The company raised its fiscal 2026 revenue and EPS outlook, reported stronger cash flow, and announced leadership changes as it works to deepen engagement with large customers.

Rosenblatt analyst Catharine Trebnick maintained Sprinklr with a Buy and a $12 price forecast.

Also Read: Sprinklr Eyes Long-Term Growth With ‘Project Bear Hug’ Despite Customer Churn Concerns: Analyst

Trebnick said Sprinklr delivered a strong second-quarter with revenue of $212 million, up 8% Y/Y and above both guidance and consensus, supported by 6% growth in subscriptions and 22% growth in professional services. She noted that the operating margin held steady at 18%, cash flow exceeded expectations, and management raised fiscal 2026 revenue and EPS guidance as the number of customers generating over $1 million in annual revenue grew.

She highlighted leadership changes, including Scott Millard joining as CRO and CFO Manish Sarin’s upcoming departure, with CEO Rory Read stepping in as interim CFO. Despite these strong results and guidance raises, Trebnick said shares fell about 8% post-earnings, reflecting investor unease over executive transitions and ongoing customer churn. Still, she reiterated a Buy rating with a $12 price forecast, pointing to improvement among large customers and progress with Project Bear Hug, which aims to deepen engagement with top accounts.

Trebnick lifted her fiscal 2026 revenue forecast to $838 million (from $826 million) and adjusted EPS estimate to $0.43 (from $0.40), above Street consensus. For the second quarter, she pointed out Sprinklr’s operating income of $38.2 million (18% margin) beat her estimate of $33.9 million, and adjusted EPS of $0.13 topped consensus at $0.10.

Trebnick added that while near-term margins face pressure from AI and R&D investments, stronger-than-expected revenue supports the raised full-year EPS outlook.

Price Action: CXM shares are down 0.84% at $7.67 at the last check on Thursday.

  • Science Applications Trims Sales Outlook As CEO Highlights Delays

Photo: Shutterstock

data.title: Ripple's Foray Into Africa's Stablecoin Market Could Replace Tether's USDT With RLUSD
data.description:
In its efforts to make the RLUSD stablecoin globally available, Ripple XRP/USD has partnered with Africa-based companies to provide it to institutions in Africa.

What Happened: Ripple is bringing its USD-backed stablecoin RLUSD to Africa through new partnerships with fintech leaders Chipper Cash, VALR, and Yellow Card.

The launch aims to strengthen cross-border payments, treasury operations, and institutional blockchain adoption across the continent.

Since its debut in late 2024, RLUSD has grown to a more than $700 million market cap, positioning itself as a compliance-first, enterprise-grade stablecoin under New York regulatory oversight.

Ripple touts RLUSD as a trusted bridge between traditional finance and crypto, designed to support large-scale institutional use.

For its Africa rollout:

  • Chipper Cash will leverage RLUSD for instant, cheaper cross-border payments,
  • VALR plans to expand trusted stablecoin listings on its exchange,
  • Yellow Card is integrating RLUSD into payment infrastructure for emerging markets.

Ripple emphasized RLUSD's utility across use cases like settlement, remittances, liquidity access, DeFi integration, fiat-crypto bridging, and even tokenizing real-world assets such as commodities and securities.

Tether’s USDT USDT/USD is dominant in Africa, as it processes more than half of the total transactions in the country, CryptoSlate reported.

Also Read: XRP Hovers Below $3: When Is The Big Breakout Coming?

Why It Matters: Ripple is also piloting RLUSD for humanitarian finance in Kenya.

In partnership with Mercy Corps Ventures, it is testing climate insurance payouts for farmers, with smart contracts automatically releasing funds based on satellite weather data.

Beyond Africa, RLUSD is available on major global exchanges including Kraken, Gemini, Bitstamp, Bitso, Mercado Bitcoin, and Uphold. Ripple sees RLUSD as a worldwide stablecoin standard built on compliance and institutional trust.

In a related announcement, Ripple projected that 10% of global assets could be tokenized by 2030, with digital asset custody expected to reach $16 trillion, a trend that could further accelerate stablecoin adoption.

  • Alphabet’s 2026 Google Cloud Universal Ledger Could Rival Ripple’s XRP Ledger — Here’s What We Know

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Here's How Much You Would Have Made Owning Carpenter Technology Stock In The Last 15 Years
data.description:
Carpenter Technology CRS has outperformed the market over the past 15 years by 1.54% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 13.96%. Currently, Carpenter Technology has a market capitalization of $11.75 billion.

Buying $100 In CRS: If an investor had bought $100 of CRS stock 15 years ago, it would be worth $723.56 today based on a price of $235.72 for CRS at the time of writing.

Carpenter Technology's Performance Over Last 15 Years

data.title: Trump's Tariffs Are Hitting America Harder Than Its Rivals, Trade Data Show
data.description:
President Donald Trump‘s aggressive use of tariffs to shrink the U.S. trade gap is falling short, as fresh data show the trade deficit just hit its worst monthly level since March despite rising duties on foreign goods.

In July, U.S. goods and services trade deficit widened to $78.3 billion, up from $59.1 billion in June, according to the Bureau of Economic Analysis.

That was worse than economists' expectations of a $75.7 billion shortfall and marked the largest monthly trade gap since March 2025.

The trade deficit is now virtually unchanged from a year ago, signaling that Trump's tariffs—aimed at cutting imports and protecting American industries—have yet to yield meaningful results.

Chart: US Trade Deficit Remains Deep In The Red

Imports Surge While Export Growth Stalls

The worsening U.S. trade gap in July was driven almost entirely by a sharp rise in imports, which climbed 5.9% from June to $358.8 billion, while exports rose a modest 0.3% to $280.5 billion. Exports of goods increased by just $0.2 billion to $179.4 billion.

Gains were led by nonmonetary gold, which rose by $2.9 billion, along with computer accessories and civilian aircraft, which increased by $0.9 billion and $0.7 billion, respectively.

On the services side, exports rose $0.6 billion to $101.0 billion, driven by stronger revenues from charges for the use of intellectual property and government-related goods and services.

However, declines were seen in categories such as finished metal shapes, which fell by $2.5 billion, and excavating machinery, down $1.5 billion.

On the import side, the story was markedly different. Imports of goods soared by $18.4 billion to reach $283.3 billion, with major increases across several product categories.

Purchases of nonmonetary gold surged $9.6 billion, while industrial supplies and materials added another $12.5 billion. Imports of computers and telecommunications equipment also increased, up $1.5 billion and $0.9 billion, respectively. Yet not all categories saw gains.

The U.S. imported fewer semiconductors, passenger cars and pharmaceutical preparations, which fell by $0.8 billion, $0.8 billion, and $1.1 billion, respectively. Imports of services also rose, increasing $1.7 billion to $75.5 billion, largely on the back of higher travel and transport activity.

Which Countries Are Driving The Deficit?

The largest contributors to the U.S. trade deficit in July included Mexico, with a shortfall of $16.6 billion, followed by Vietnam at $16.1 billion, China at $14.7 billion, and Taiwan at $13.5 billion.

Deficits were also substantial with other trading partners. The European Union posted an $8.6 billion gap, India ran a $5.5 billion deficit, and Canada followed closely at $5.4 billion.

A notable development came from the widening deficit with China, which increased by $5.3 billion compared to June. U.S. imports from China surged to $24.7 billion, while exports remained flat at $10.0 billion.

The deficit with Switzerland also spiked significantly, jumping from nearly zero to $7.7 billion, as imports rose $6.5 billion—primarily driven by gold-related transactions—while exports fell by $1.2 billion to $2.9 billion.

The U.S. posted trade surpluses with a few regions. The Netherlands showed a positive balance of $4.8 billion, South and Central America contributed a $4.6 billion surplus, and Hong Kong added $1.9 billion to the plus side of the ledger.

Read now:

  • Trump: Without Tariffs, ‘We’re Not Going To Have A Country’

Photo: Shutterstock

data.title: Over $120 Million Pours Into AI Startups Across New Frontiers
data.description:
The AI Funding Boom Continues Across Diverse Sectors

The artificial intelligence sector continues to attract significant venture capital despite broader economic uncertainties, with the past week seeing over $120 million invested across a diverse range of AI startups. From healthcare to construction, specialized AI applications are drawing investor attention as the technology’s transformative potential becomes increasingly apparent across industries.

Healthcare and Biotech: AI’s Next Frontier

Perhaps one of the most exciting and impactful areas of AI investment lies in biotech. The global AI in biotechnology market, valued at $2.73 billion in 2023, stood at $3.23 billion in 2024, and is projected to advance at a resilient CAGR of 19.1% from 2024 to 2029, culminating in a forecasted valuation of $7.75 billion by the end of the period.

Source: Markets and Markets

Therna Biosciences’ Approach to AI in the Body

San Francisco-based Therna Biosciences secured $10 million in a seed round from Fusion Fund, Pear VC, and one other investor as a testament to AI’s ability in analyzing complex biological data, simulating molecular interactions, and accelerating research cycles.

Barti and the Future of Care Management

Another San Francisco-based AI startup, Barti, secured $12 million in a Series A round from Bienville Capital, Zag Capital, and one other investor. The company specializes in AI for care management, pioneering AI-driven tools for coordinating care for the elderly or chronically ill, personalizing health plans, predicting health declines, or assisting caregivers.

Enterprise Solutions: Transforming Business Operations

The AI applications for enterprise environments market is a rapidly growing sector, driven by the increasing adoption of AI for data analysis, automation, personalized customer experiences, and risk management across diverse industries. The global enterprise artificial intelligence market size was estimated at $23.95 billion in 2024 and is projected to reach $155.2 billion by 2030, growing at a CAGR of 37.6% from 2025 to 2030.

Pageport’s AI-Powered Customer Relationships

Pageport, based in American Fork, Utah, secured $3.3 million from 2048 Ventures and Matchstick Ventures for its AI-enhanced CRM solution. The company’s technology promises to revolutionize customer relationship management by automating data entry and providing deeper insights into customer interactions. By integrating AI into CRM systems, Pageport aims to provide a deeper, more predictive understanding of customer behavior, leading to more personalized interactions and improved sales outcomes.

ALIGNMT AI for AI Management

ALIGNMT AI from New York City raised $6.5 million from Dent Capital, Alumni Ventures, and two additional investors. As AI proliferates, so does the need for its responsible management, where the company's AI management solutions step in, helping enterprises deploy and govern artificial intelligence systems effectively across their organizations. This investment acknowledges the critical importance of ensuring AI systems are fair, transparent, secure, and compliant with evolving regulations.

Mantic Technologies and the Power of Data Analytics

London-based Mantic Technologies Ltd secured $4 million from Episode 1 and DRW Venture Capital, reinforcing the eternal truth: AI is only as good as the data it’s fed. Their focus on AI and data and analytics speaks to the fundamental need for robust, intelligent data infrastructure. As AI models become more sophisticated, the demand for cleaning, processing, and deriving insights from increasingly vast datasets will only intensify. This investment, while perhaps less flashy than some of its counterparts, represents a critical foundational layer for the entire AI ecosystem. 

Specialized AI Applications Gaining Traction

Source: AI-Generated by Andre Bourque

Some of the most interesting recent investments have gone to companies developing highly specialized AI applications. 

Bench IQ’s AI in Corporate Law

Bench IQ, based in San Francisco, raised $5.3 million from Roach Capital, Battery Ventures, and five other investors to advance its AI platform for corporate law, mirroring prior AI investments in this sector. By applying AI to corporate law, the company aims to automate research, analyze legal documents, predict case outcomes, and potentially even draft legal arguments. This demonstrates AI’s ability to democratize access to expertise, reduce costs, and improve efficiency in traditionally high-cost, knowledge-intensive sectors.

Reframe Systems and AI in Construction

Reframe Systems from Andover secured an impressive $20 million from Nor’easter Ventures, RA Capital Management, and five additional investors. The company is applying AI to the construction industry, using machine learning to optimize building designs, reduce material waste, and improve project management. This highlights AI’s expansion into industries traditionally less associated with high-tech innovation. AI in construction could encompass everything from project planning and resource optimization to predictive maintenance of machinery, safety monitoring, and even robotic construction. 

Eyebot’s AI in Eyewear

Boston-based Eyebot also raised $20 million from Ravelin Capital, Ubiquity Ventures, and five other investors. The company has AI-powered solutions for custom fitting, design, and manufacturing of glasses and contact lenses.

The Rise of Agentic AI and AI Agents

A particularly notable trend is the growing investment in agentic AI, the next evolution in artificial intelligence autonomous systems capable of performing complex tasks with minimal human intervention. These systems go beyond simple automation to actually make decisions and take actions based on high-level goals.

Maisa AI Leading the Autonomous Charge

San Francisco-based Maisa AI secured $25 million from Creandum, NFX, and three other investors to develop its agentic AI platform. The company's focus on agentic AI suggests a move beyond mere automation to truly autonomous, goal-oriented AI systems capable of complex decision-making and continuous learning.

Loman and the Human-like Interface

Similarly, Loman from Austin raised $3.5 million from Antler, TenOneTen Ventures, and another investor for its AI agent technology. The company's solution typifies a broader wave enabling AI to serve as personal and business assistants across industries, further embedding AI in everyday workflows.

Infrastructure and Enabling Technologies

Astrus and AI in Analog IC Design

Not all AI investments are focused on end-user applications. Toronto-based Astrus secured CA11 million (approximately $S8.1 million) from Bluesky Equities, Inovia Capital, and three other investors. The company specializes in analog IC (integrated circuit) design for AI applications, developing the hardware infrastructure that enables more efficient AI processing for what is otherwise  notoriously complex and labor-intensive. By applying AI to this field, Astrus is poised to significantly accelerate design cycles, improve performance, and reduce costs in a critical component of virtually all electronic devices.

Democratizing AI Development

Skillmaker.AI and the “AI as a Service” Revolution

North Carolina-based Skillmaker.AI secured $2.5 million from First Talent Ventures, Cape Fear Ventures, and five other investors. As AI becomes more ubiquitous, so does the need for accessible tools and platforms for businesses to leverage its power without deep in-house expertise. Skillmaker.AI's focus on “AI as a Service” (AIaaS) highlights the burgeoning market for readily deployable AI solutions. This investment isn’t just about a single product; it’s a vote of confidence in the future where AI is consumed like a utility, integrated seamlessly into existing workflows, and empowering a broader range of enterprises.

What These Investments Tell Us About the Future of AI

Source: AI-Generated by Andre Bourque

The diversity of recent AI investments reveals several important trends shaping the industry:

  1. Vertical specialization: Rather than general-purpose AI platforms, investors are increasingly backing startups focused on specific industries or use cases.
  2. Enterprise adoption acceleration: Many funded startups are building solutions that help traditional businesses implement AI more effectively.
  3. Infrastructure investment: The continued development of AI requires advances in hardware and enabling technologies, attracting significant investment.
  4. Autonomous systems: Funding for agentic AI and autonomous agents suggests growing confidence in more independent AI systems.
  5. Geographic diversity: While Silicon Valley remains an AI hub, significant investments are flowing to startups across North America and Europe.

The Road Ahead

As AI continues to mature, investors are becoming more discerning, looking for startups that can demonstrate clear differentiation and viable paths to profitability. The days of securing funding based solely on incorporating AI into a business model are waning. Investors are asking tougher questions about how AI startups will monetize their technology and scale effectively. While there’s still tremendous enthusiasm for AI, more and more it’s coupled with higher expectations for business fundamentals.

Feature Image AI-Generated by Andre Bourque

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.
